text: Put it out, and a light of the same brightness comes on somewhere deeper in the tunnels.
The needle always points down. The repairman insists it is not broken.
You could swear it keeps growing new faces, but it never counts past six.
The portrait inside is blank. Day by day, the blank looks more like someone.
Count it and the total changes. On days it increases, it is said to be in a good mood.
The first blade any delver ever holds.

column3: but it hits back just as hard.

text: A well-forged edge that still gleams in the dark.
%d ticks passed while you were away

Day three. The soil here is soft. The more you dig, the more you earn — good work, honest work. But the foreman says strange things. "Train your ears before you train your depth," he tells us. Whatever that means. Last night, from somewhere far below the shaft, came a sound — not quite water, not quite breathing. An aquifer, everyone laughed. I laughed too.

Dear brother. Thank you for the money. The site here is the same as ever. There is just one odd rule: "Any stone tablet you find, hand it in unread." They say the ones who read them are the ones who quit. The others laugh — you can't even read, you're the safest man in the pit. ...But brother. If a man cannot read, how is it that in his dreams he finds himself *reading* it anyway?

[LEGIBLE SECTION] ...to appease it, we ceased our digging. Seven times we filled the shaft back in, and seven times we failed. Return the soil, stack the stones — it does not matter. The depth remembers. You may forget, but the hole does not. ...[REMAINDER LOST]

I am beginning to understand what the foreman means by training your ears. Deep down, the sound of your pickaxe comes back to you — one beat late, and somehow a little more polite. I took it for an echo at first. Then Yorek tried it: three strikes, then stop. It answered with four. The foreman said: "Don't try to match its count. As long as it matches yours, the work goes well." And in fact, they say this month's yield is the highest on record.
Torn Page from a Wage Ledger

[Ledger, excerpt] Week 7: Daron, 20 silver. Yorek, 20 silver. Mim, 25 (night shift). Week 8: Daron, 40. Yorek, 40. Note: wages doubled for all work below the tenth stratum. Week 9: Daron, 80. Note: wages quadrupled. Held in storage, recipient absent. Week 10: Note: we are running out of storage.

We reached the aquifer today. The water is clean, and cold, and it flows upward. That is not a slip of the pen. It climbs the walls in quiet, orderly lines, up toward the ceiling. Daron said there must be a lake on top of the mountain. We laughed. I have noticed, lately, that we measure our fear by how often we laugh. Today we laughed a great deal.

[NOTICE No. 11] The following are prohibited: 1. Singing in the shafts. 2. Whistling in the shafts. 3. Deliberately breaking the rhythm of the work. 4. Striking back in reply to the Answer. 5. Explaining the reasons for the above when a newcomer asks. — Those who know the reasons already know how to answer. — The Foreman

[Royal Bureau of Mines — Charter] The 170th petition to resume excavation is hereby GRANTED, on the following conditions: the workforce shall not exceed one hundred; no open flame shall be used below the fortieth stratum; the decision to withdraw rests entirely with the site foreman, and the capital need not be notified until the withdrawal is complete. — Note: the Bureau is obliged to record that all one hundred and sixty-nine previous petitions were written in the same hand.

(Scratched into the bedrock wall with a nail, or something like one) HERE SLEEPS MIM. a lie. not sleeping. awake. always awake. keep quiet and the work is easy. don't sing. don't count. don't answer. — And beneath that, one line in a different hand: "Mim is doing fine. Please don't worry."

We were not the first. In the thirty-fourth stratum we broke into scaffolding that was not ours, and the ruins of an archive that was not ours. The tree rings in the timber date it to twelve hundred years ago. Here the journal changes. The pages that follow are in our own surveyor's handwriting — but in letters none of us know. Only the final line can be read: "The rest is below."

[Inscription in the stone chamber] The digger shall be filled. That is the promise of this place. Soil into silver, silver doubled, the double doubled again. Seek the depth. Yield not the depth. ...Here the inscription breaks off, scraped away. On examination, the tool that scraped it was the same tool that carved it.

Yorek quit. To be precise, he stopped coming to morning roll. His pack is still here. His bunk is still here. His wage pouch is still here. The foreman sent out no search party. All he said was: "Those who go down are employed down there." I took it for a joke, and laughed. Nobody laughed. Since this month, for some reason, there are two wage pouches with my name on them.

Dear brother. You will recall I wrote that I cannot read. I can now. I do not remember learning. One morning I woke, and the marks on the tablets looked like plain dictation — just sounds, written down. They say it happens to everyone here. One of the old hands puts it differently: "You haven't learned the tunnel's language. It has learned yours." Either way, the pay is good. Only — these days, in my dreams, I find I am writing my letters to you in those marks.

Carved into the stone wall, a flower about the size of a palm. Five petals. This is the third time I've seen this exact pattern today — the other two were nowhere near here. Same mason's habit, or the same era's fashion? The foreman just shook his head when I asked. But the depth of the carving is identical, to the hair's width, all three times.

In the margin of the patrol log, one line in careful handwriting: "Moribito — passage confirmed." The date is two hundred years before today. The log itself records nothing unusual for that day. Who confirmed what, and to whom? The same handwriting turns up more often the further back the records go.

We were pressing our ears to the rock for the survey. Mim said she heard a bell. No one else could. Only Mim, she said. The foreman told her to see a doctor, but by the next day three more of us said we'd heard it too. Now the ones who can't hear it are the minority. I still can't.

People come to this site from the east and from the west. The words are different, but the work-signals somehow carry across. No one taught them to, but everyone swings the hammer to the same beat. The other day I heard four men from four different countries humming — the words were all different. Only the tune was the same.

Borrowed a ten-year-old survey map and laid it over today's shaft. The numbers don't line up. The depth on record is always a little shallower than what we've actually dug — as if the bottom drifts a little further away every time we reach it. The foreman just said, "That's how it is down here."

The reply arrived before I filed the requisition. Its date is three days before I submitted mine. What it said matched, word for word, what I was about to write. I hadn't told anyone. No one had asked me.

I laid two documents side by side. One is this month's supply permit. The other is a decommission order from forty years ago, found in the back of the storeroom. Different senders, different departments. But the wax seal stamped on both — down to where it's chipped — is identical.

Once every ten days, a peddler comes through with his cart. Decent stock. Every time he calls out, it's the same line: "A fine find for you today!" — that's all he ever says, and yet somehow, every time, he already has exactly what I wanted.

Four small talismans turned up under the rubble. Different craft, different script, all four from different countries. An eastern knot, a western crest, southern lettering, a mountain-folk carving. No one can explain why things from four separate nations ended up buried in the same spot.

[Fixed-point temperature survey, shaft interior] Fifty years ago, the record shows twelve degrees Celsius at the fortieth stratum. Measured at the same spot today: fifteen. It should get colder with depth, not warmer. The engineer wrote "geothermal, probably," then crossed the line out and wrote "unknown" instead.

We opened a new shaft today, five to a crew. Someone said five was lucky. Someone else insisted five was bad luck, that it should be four or six. Neither of them could say why. In the end the foreman just left it at five. Nothing happened, the first day.

[Draft — Mine Closure Notice] The reason field says "state the cause below." In the previous clerk's draft, instead of a reason, the same two words were written thirty-seven times: "still not enough." That page was destroyed before filing.

While sorting old ledgers, I found the same name in a purchase record from forty years ago: "Anastas." No one from that era is still on site. Just a coincidence of names, everyone says. But the little sketch scrawled next to the entry looks uncannily like the peddler I met today.

The cornerstone at the shaft mouth has the founding year carved into it. The numbers are too worn to read cleanly, so we took a rubbing and checked it against the survey office's records. They didn't match. "There are re-carving marks," the engineer said. "More than once."

[Wage Payment Record] Sorting this month's payouts, I noticed something. The coin pouches used to receive wages were a genuine mix — eastern copper, western silver, Great Yan ingots, mountain-folk trade pelts. Apparently that's not unusual for this site. What's strange is how evenly split the ratio always comes out.

Heard that sound again tonight. The first time, on the third night, everyone laughed it off as an aquifer. Half a year since then, and the nights I hear it now outnumber the nights I don't. The notice says not to count them. I count them anyway. This month makes nine.

A small bell turned up in the rubble. Its clapper is missing — shake it and nothing sounds. But the night we brought it back, Mim said she woke up to the sound of a bell ringing. It still hasn't made a sound since.

The rest of that patrol log turned up on a different shelf. The same note appeared again, this time on a page from fifty years back: "Moribito — passage confirmed." Same placement on the page, same use of the margin, down to the smallest detail. As if the same hand had written it across generations.

Some of the traveling merchants call this pit "Abyssos." When I asked about the unfamiliar word, all I got was: "Old language. Means something like 'the depths,' I'm told." No one on site calls it that. Just "the pit" — that's enough for everyone here.

One of the young mountain-folk workers showed me a letter from home. He can't read the script himself — apparently messages are carried by the number of knots tied into the cord. Today's letter had three knots. "Means 'all is well,'" he said, smiling. He wouldn't tell me more than that.
An Older Shaft Beneath the Shaft

We broke through the wall of the current tunnel and hit another one — older, built differently. The stonework technique doesn't match ours. Whoever dug it, it predates even our earliest survey records. "Someone got here first," the engineer said, and then said nothing more.

Mim doesn't seem as frightened as she used to be. "You get used to hearing it," she says. But someone saw her smiling in her bunk at night. When asked what was funny, she wouldn't say. She just always seems to be in a good mood these days.

We wrote to the stonemasons' guild about the five-petal flower motif. The reply was short: "We have no record of that design. We know of someone who might — but we cannot give a name." The only signature was an unreadable stamp.

[Night Roll Call Record] Sometimes the roster and the actual count don't match. On the high side, that is. A low count you'd blame on desertion or accident — but a high count? The counter usually just writes it off as a miscount. But the nights the count runs high always seem to be the same nights that sound is loudest.
This is an idle game. It keeps digging while you work — come back whenever.

Scoop water with it, and there is less water afterward than before.
The reflection blinks a beat after you do.
It fits every lock, but every door it opens leads to the same room.
Leave the pair together, and by morning both are right hands.
Turn it over and the sand does not fall. The day ends anyway.
Press it into anything, and only the mark stays warm for a while.
Blow it once, and a second note answers from deeper in the tunnels a moment later.
The pages stay blank, yet the diary gains one more sheet every time it is closed.
No hands, no face -- yet held to the ear, it ticks with perfect timing.
Whatever you put in, the weight never changes. No one who has looked inside has come back yet.
A Contract of a Single Signature
Every clause is blank; only the list of signatures keeps growing.
Buried in the strata, yet the moment it is dug up, it keeps growing toward the light.
Pin something with it, and your memory of that thing fades a little.
Someone is said to still be digging for its pair. The tunnels have not given it back.
It is damp, yet strike it and it lights, exactly once, every time.
The destination is blank, but hold it tight and you always arrive somewhere.
Open the seal and the words are always written on the other side.
Pour water in, and the ground beneath your feet grows damp by the same amount.
Count the beads and the total is always one off from last time.
Light it and the wax flows upward, pooling above the flame.
A Wallet of Nothing But IOUs
Open it and there is a new IOU inside each time, though no one can read the signature.
Empty it completely, and closing the lid fills it again.
Hold it to your ear and you hear not the sea, but the sound of deep tunnels.
Only one lot in the box, yet it is still there each time you draw.
None of the roads on it lead outside the tunnels, yet new ones are added daily.
It never cracks, yet you can feel it beating.
Wear one, and somewhere far off the other glows on the same finger.
The painting is gone, yet the right light still traces someone's outline.
Slip it into any book, and it always falls open to the final page.
Wear them and your footsteps vanish, though footprints keep appearing after you take them off.
A Letter Clip That Will Not Open
The papers clipped inside keep multiplying; no one remembers how to remove them.
Found deep underground, yet no amount of drying removes the smell of the sea.
An Offering Box With No Weight
Drop coins in, and the box's weight from outside never changes.
Only a shadow appears in the photo, but the light's direction is different every time.
A Bell That Answers When Called
Call a name and it rings once, though whose name it answers to is unclear.
A Cocoon That Will Not Crush
No amount of force crushes it. You can only feel something inside, waiting.
Similar to the brass compass, but this one's needle always points up.
No one presses it, yet a chime answers back from deep in the tunnels now and then.
A Drawer That Will Not Open
Only the bottom tier is ever slightly open; it is empty, but cold.
A Tablet With a Counting Song
Recite it aloud, and someone murmurs the next number for you.
An Umbrella Rib That Never Bends
The umbrella is long gone, but this rib alone still remembers the smell of rain.
A Cricket Cage With No Sound
No insect lives inside, yet it chirps faintly once night falls.
Only the top half is legible; the bottom half is a different stain each time.
A Plate Engraved on the Underside
Flip it over to check, and the engraved name looks different every time.
Bent out of shape, yet blow it and it plays the note it made before breaking.
The carving wore away long ago, yet stamped, the faded pattern faintly returns.
Untie it and it slowly works its way back into the same knot.
Held to a mirror it reads clearly, naming a place no one has heard of.
A Wick From a Lamp That Never Lights
Soak it in oil and it never lights, yet it stays faintly warm in the dark.
A Box of Gears That Do Not Add Up
Assemble every gear and one is left over. Discard it, and by morning one is left over again.
The wood it is driven into ages every year; the nail itself stays forever new.
Unstruck, yet the tip alone carries a faint warmth.
Every plan is left blank; only the dates are filled in, meticulously.
A Jar That Answers When Tapped
Tap it, and a moment later the same rhythm answers from inside.
Wherever the shards are found, arranged together they always form one jar.
A Lantern of Only a Wick
No glass, no oil, yet on windless days it flickers ever so slightly.
Tilt it and the sand moves from the bottom to the top.
A Keyring With No Sign of Use
Every key looks brand new, yet the ring holding them together is worn smooth with age.
Even cold, it dents to the shape of a fingertip when touched.
The paper is torn, but the frame alone still traces the shape of a light.
A Bell That Makes No Sound
Shake it and nothing sounds, yet a moment later, something rings deep in your ear.
Drink from the chipped side, and it always feels hotter than it should.
Use it, and the tip grows a little longer for every nail pulled.
It sharpens any blade, but by the next day the edge dulls back to where it started.
Tie it, and somewhere far off, its missing partner seems to tie itself too.
Half its teeth are missing, yet somehow it combs through hair without a single snag.
No amount of wiping clears the fog, yet distant things come sharply into focus when worn.
It cannot cut paper, but it cuts hair cleanly enough.
It fits halfway into any lock. No one knows where the other half went.
Pull it and it always feels like there is more length left, yet it stops at the same point every time.
Too crushed to wear, yet the feeling of wearing it lingers.
Polishing does not remove the tarnish, yet the reflected face is unusually clear.
Traces of grinding remain, and the smell of flour never quite fades.
Too small even for a child's hand, yet it fits an adult's hand perfectly when worn.
Only the middle of a road is drawn; neither its start nor its end can be found.
It will not pass through straight cloth, but slides easily through cloth already creased.
Held to the ear, it plays not the sound of waves, but someone's footsteps.
Long since burnt out, yet in darkness alone, its tip glows faintly.
It should shrink with every wash, yet by morning it is back to the same size.
A Worn Half of a Sandal
Only one side shows wear from walking; its match is nowhere to be found.
Try to draw a straight line with it, and the line always bends, just slightly.
Long empty, yet open the lid and the smell of medicine still lingers.
Warped out of shape, yet somehow it fits perfectly when slipped onto a finger.
A Bow That Will Not Come Undone
Try to undo the knot, and it only pulls tighter.
Ring it from the chipped side, and instead of a clear tone, it sighs like someone breathing out.
The tip has long gone round, yet whatever it writes still looks sharply pointed.
Empty inside, yet the spot where it was once sealed is still warm.
Hold it straight, and by the time it reaches your mouth, the angle has always shifted.
Wear it, and you recall even the weight of the one that used to match it.
Something like writing is carved into it, though no one can read it.
Every page is blank, yet opening it always carries the smell of fresh ink.
Even in the cold tunnels, it keeps slowly losing its shape.
Batteries do nothing, yet holding it, your hand warms just slightly.
Only the final measure of notes can be read; no one knows the rest of the song.
Open the lid and it is empty, yet closing it always makes a small sound.
Hurls a ball of fire at the enemy
The party was overwhelmed and retreated
